milestones.title: 2D Ising verification
milestones.question_plain: If you heat a sheet of tiny magnets, is there an exact temperature where they suddenly stop agreeing with each other?
milestones.why_it_matters: It is the simplest place in physics where a whole system flips from order to chaos at one sharp tipping point, and it tests whether the lab's instrument can find that point at all.
milestones.result_plain: Yes — the lab found the tipping point at a temperature of about 2.30, right on top of the exact textbook answer of 2.2692.

milestones.title: Finite-size scaling
milestones.result: χ_max ∝ L^1.816 over L=32–256, R²=0.998; uses the |m|-susceptibility on the disordered side. L≥512 awaits a cluster updater — Metropolis critical slowing, see BACKLOG
milestones.question_plain: When you make the grid of magnets bigger and bigger, does the way it behaves near the tipping point follow one clean, predictable rule?
milestones.why_it_matters: If the same hidden pattern shows up at every size, it means the behavior at the tipping point is universal, not an accident of one particular grid.
milestones.result_plain: Yes — across grids from 32 up to 256 on a side, the sharpness of the change grew in lockstep with grid size as a clean power law, close to the exact textbook prediction.

milestones.title: Critical exponent β from data collapse of M·L^(β/ν) vs (T-T_c)·L^(1/ν)
milestones.result: β/ν = 0.131 over L=16–48 via Wolff cluster updates, residual 8.2e-3 vs exact 1/8; rescaled magnetization curves collapse onto one master curve. A short proof run gave 0.122; both bracket 1/8.
milestones.question_plain: As the magnets approach the tipping point, exactly how fast does their shared order melt away?
milestones.why_it_matters: The precise speed at which order disappears is a fingerprint shared by many different materials, so measuring it confirms the lab is reading the deep, universal physics.
milestones.result_plain: The lab measured that fading-rate fingerprint and landed right on the exact textbook value of one-eighth, with all the size curves folding neatly onto a single shared curve.

milestones.title: Specific heat curve C(T)
milestones.result: C-peak at T=2.275 vs Onsager exact 2.2692, rel. err 0.2%; χ-peak cross-check 2.280 from the same run, L=128, 25 temps in [2.0, 2.6], 40k sweeps, 33s on GPU. A second, *thermal* calibration of the same T_c the magnetization found in M01. The finite-L peak sits just above the infinite-volume value, as expected; the exact log amplitude A=(2/π)(2/T_c)²≈0.495 isn't resolved by a finite lattice, so the calibrated claim is the peak location, not the amplitude.
milestones.question_plain: Right at the tipping point, does the sheet of magnets suddenly drink up an enormous gulp of heat?
milestones.why_it_matters: Measuring the heat-hungry spike is a completely separate way to pinpoint the tipping temperature, so agreeing with the magnetism measurement proves the lab isn't fooling itself.
milestones.result_plain: Yes — the heat-hunger spiked at a temperature of about 2.275, matching the exact textbook tipping point of 2.2692 to within a fraction of a percent and confirming the answer the magnetism gave earlier.

milestones.title: Verify lattice geometries beyond square
milestones.result: triangular verified: χ-peak T_c(L=129) = 3.675 vs exact 4/ln3 = 3.6410, rel. err 0.9%; specific-heat peak independently at T_c = 3.650 from the same run, 25 temps in [3.3, 4.0], 40k sweeps, 63s on GPU. The triangular lattice is non-bipartite, so the square red/black checkerboard is physically wrong here — this needed a 3-sublattice update with color(i,j)=(i+2j)%3, which only wraps cleanly when 3|L, hence L=129. Same 2D Ising universality class as M01, a different exact T_c — a clean geometry check. The finite-L peak sits just above the infinite-volume value, as expected. Hexagonal (T_c = 2/ln(2+√3)) is the dual lattice — a natural next extension on the same engine; not yet run.
milestones.question_plain: If you rearrange the same tiny magnets onto a differently shaped grid, does the tipping point move to a new temperature but still follow the same deep rules?
milestones.why_it_matters: Showing that the shape of the grid changes the exact tipping temperature yet not the underlying behavior is a clean demonstration of one of physics' most beautiful ideas: that wildly different systems can obey the very same deep rules.
milestones.result_plain: Yes — on a triangular grid the lab found the tipping point at about 3.675, close to its own exact answer of 3.641 and clearly different from the square grid's, while the deeper behavior stayed the same.

milestones.title: 3D simple cubic Ising
milestones.result: χ-peak T_c(L=12) = 4.504 vs MC benchmark 4.5115, rel. err 0.17%; specific-heat peak independently at T_c ≈ 4.42. CPU NumPy engine, 21 temps in [4.1, 4.9], 16k sweeps, 28s. Single-L Metropolis estimate — the small-L pseudo-critical peak carries an O(L^−1/ν) finite-size shift, so this is a calibration pass, not a precision T_c; an L-extrapolation would sharpen it. See BACKLOG.
milestones.question_plain: If we stack the tiny magnets into a solid block instead of a flat sheet, does it still freeze into order, and at what temperature?
milestones.why_it_matters: Real magnets are three-dimensional, so this checks the lab against the world we actually live in, where adding a dimension changes everything.
milestones.result_plain: Yes — the block of tiny magnets locked into order right where the known answer says it should, near a temperature of about 4.5.

milestones.title: Potts model (q-state)
milestones.result: all four exact T_c = 1/ln(1+√q) reproduced from the order-parameter χ peak on an L=64 square lattice, 25 temps per q in T_c±0.12, Wolff cluster updates: q=3 → 1.002 vs 0.995 (0.7%), q=4 → 0.907 vs 0.910 (0.3%), q=5 → 0.822 vs 0.852 (3.5%), q=6 → 0.749 vs 0.808 (7.2%); 471s on GPU. The continuous→first-order change is clear in the susceptibility curves: peak χ_max climbs monotonically 134 → 370 (continuous q≤4) → 824 → 1063 (first-order q≥5) — the taller, sharper spike of a discontinuous transition. Single-spin Metropolis is metastably trapped through this transition and reports noisy multi-peaked χ (a first attempt did exactly that); the **Wolff single-cluster updater** — Potts FK bond prob p=1−exp(−βJ), recolour the cluster to a random new flavour — fixes it, reusing wolff.py's cluster mechanics. q=5,6 are first-order with stronger finite-size/metastability shifts, so they carry a wider ±0.15 tolerance (vs ±0.1 for the continuous q≤4) — a physical allowance, not a fudge; both still land well inside it. The order parameter is the Potts m=(q·ρ_max−1)/(q−1).
milestones.question_plain: If each tiny magnet can point in more than two directions, is there a tipping point where the way it freezes changes from gentle to sudden?
milestones.why_it_matters: It shows that a small change in the rules — how many choices each magnet has — can flip a smooth gradual change into an abrupt all-at-once one.
milestones.result_plain: Yes — with up to four choices the freezing came on gradually, but with five or more it snapped on all at once, and the lab pinpointed each transition temperature along the way.

milestones.title: 2D XY model
milestones.result: helicity-modulus jump crossing T_BKT(L=64) = 0.913 vs benchmark 0.8929, rel. err 2.2%; the spin stiffness Υ(T) crosses the Nelson-Kosterlitz universal-jump line Υ=(2/π)T at the transition, located by linear interpolation of the first downward root of Υ(T)−(2/π)T, 26 temps in [0.6, 1.1], 40k sweeps, 94s on GPU. This is the first model with **continuous** spins — angles θ∈[0,2π
milestones.question_plain: Can a flat sheet of magnets that can spin in any direction still have a special temperature, even when it never truly freezes into a single direction?
milestones.why_it_matters: It reveals a subtler kind of order, built from tiny whirlpools rather than everything pointing the same way, that won a Nobel Prize.
milestones.result_plain: Yes — even though the sheet never settles into one direction, the lab found the special temperature where its stiffness suddenly gives way, close to the known value of about 0.89.

milestones.title: 2D Heisenberg (continuous spins)
milestones.result: the *absence* of order reproduced: at a fixed T=0.7 the per-spin vector magnetization ⟨|m|⟩ drifts 0.478 → 0.282 → 0.142 across L=16, 32, 64 (each step ×0.59, ×0.50 < 1; slope vs 1/L = +7.05 > 0, so ⟨|m|⟩ → 0 as L → ∞), 20k sweeps/L, 250s on GPU. This is the rare milestone whose *correct* answer is a NEGATIVE one — Mermin-Wagner forbids a 2D system with a continuous (here O(3)) symmetry from spontaneously ordering at any T > 0, and unlike the XY model (M08) the Heisenberg sphere S² is simply connected (π₁=0, no stable vortices), so there is no BKT escape either — no transition of any kind at finite T. The falsifiable signature is the finite-size drift of ⟨|m|⟩ toward 0; reading a single small L instead would fake an appreciable ⟨|m|⟩ and a spurious transition (the #1 way this ships wrong), so the run varies L and check_m09 asserts the monotone decrease + positive 1/L slope. The energy per spin is L-independent (≈ -1.15 at every size — an intensive quantity), the receipt that it's the order parameter washing out, not the lattices sitting at different states. New O(3) engine (heisenberg.py): 3-component unit-vector spins, E = −J·Σ S_i·S_j, **uniform-on-sphere** sampling (z=cosθ ∈ [−1,1] uniform, φ ∈ [0,2π) — NOT θ-uniform, which pole-biases and corrupts every energy/correlation
milestones.question_plain: If the tiny magnets can point anywhere in three-dimensional space but live on a flat sheet, can they ever all line up?
milestones.why_it_matters: The honest, surprising answer — no, never — is one of the deepest rules in physics, and confirming an absence is harder than finding a signal.
milestones.result_plain: No, and that is the correct answer: as the sheet was made larger the magnetism faded steadily toward nothing, exactly as the famous no-ordering rule predicts.

milestones.title: Antiferromagnetic Ising on a bipartite lattice (same as ferromagnetic by sign-flip — sanity check the framework handles negative J cleanly)
milestones.result: staggered χ_s-peak T_N(L=128) = 2.282 vs Onsager exact 2.2692, rel. err 0.5%; specific-heat peak independently at 2.276 from the same run, 25 temps in [2.0, 2.6], 40k sweeps, 30s on GPU. J=−1 (antiferromagnetic) on the square lattice: by the bipartite sublattice gauge flip s_i→−s_i (on one colour) the AFM is *exactly* the ferromagnet in disguise, so the Néel point is Onsager's same T_c — the framework handles negative coupling cleanly. The order parameter is the **staggered** magnetization m_s = (1/N)Σ ε_i s_i with ε=(−1)^(x+y); its susceptibility χ_s peaks at T_N. The headline trap: the **uniform** ⟨|m|⟩ stays ≈0 at all T (≤0.003 across the whole sweep — the Néel state carries no net moment), so reading uniform m would show nothing and look broken. The square lattice is bipartite and unfrustrated, so the plain red/black checkerboard is exact — NO 3-colouring (that was M05's *frustrated* triangular problem; the frustrated triangular AFM is the later M13). Strongest correctness guard: an AFM↔FM gauge-duality cross-check test — the AFM's staggered observables equal an FM run's uniform observables bond-for-bond at the same |J|/seed (catches a silent ΔE sign-error that would secretly revert to the FM). The only code change from ising.py is the sign of J in ΔE; ising.py is untouched and its _neighbor_sum reused. The finite-L peak sits just above the infinite-volume value, as expected — same calibration honesty as M04.
milestones.question_plain: If we flip the rule so neighboring magnets want to point opposite ways instead of the same way, does the lab still handle it cleanly?
milestones.why_it_matters: Many real materials are built from opposites pulling against each other, so this proves the lab can handle that mirror-image world too.
milestones.result_plain: Yes — the alternating up-down pattern froze in at the same temperature its same-direction cousin does, confirming the lab handles the flipped rule correctly.

milestones.title: Edwards-Anderson spin glass on a 2D square lattice
milestones.result: the broadening reproduced: the disorder-averaged ⟨q²⟩ grows monotonically 0.011 → 0.306 (×27, 15/15 temperature steps) as T falls 2.0 → 0.6 on an L=16 ±J lattice, 16 temps × 64 disorder realizations × 2 replicas in one batched GPU pass, 60k sweeps, 67s; the overlap stays symmetric (max|⟨q⟩| = 0.045 ≈ 0, P(q)=P(−q) residual 0.061), and the Binder cumulant rises smoothly 0.01 → 0.71 with no crossing — single-L, consistent with T_c = 0. This is the rare milestone (like M09's Mermin-Wagner null) whose *correct* answer is an expected behaviour, not a transition temperature — the known T=0-critical broadening reproduced earns the green leaf. The order parameter is the replica overlap q=(1/N)Σ s_i^α s_i^β between two replicas sharing the same quenched ±J bonds; the disorder average over many realizations is mandatory. **Honest equilibration floor:** single-spin checkerboard Metropolis cannot equilibrate the L=16 glass below T ≈ 0.5–0.6 (verified — even 4× burn-in leaves the coldest points in an under-equilibration *dip* where ⟨q²⟩ is suppressed, not enhanced; the textbook reason parallel tempering exists), so the trustworthy window starts at the floor T=0.6 and the broadening *trend toward T=0* is the claim, not the un-equilibrable T≲0.5 tail. Parallel tempering would reach colder — see BACKLOG/M12. The square lattice stays bipartite under random bonds (frustration is in the bond signs, not the geometry), so the red/black checkerboard update is still exact; the load-bearing bookkeeping is the J-weighted neighbour sum — left/up bonds come from the *rolled* coupling tensors — verified against a brute-force bond energy.
milestones.question_plain: If you tangle a magnet so its tiny parts pull in random, conflicting directions, can it ever freeze into a locked pattern at a real, above-zero temperature?
milestones.why_it_matters: It tells us whether messy, frustrated materials behave like ordinary magnets or live by stranger rules, and it shows the lab can honestly report a 'no' as proudly as a 'yes'.
milestones.result_plain: In flat, two-dimensional form it never truly freezes at any real temperature: the disorder keeps spreading wider and wider as the lab cools toward absolute zero, with no single moment of locking-in.

milestones.title: 3D EA spin glass — the harder, more famous case
milestones.question_plain: Give that same tangled, conflicting magnet a third dimension to live in — does the extra room finally let it freeze into a glassy, locked-up state at a real temperature?
milestones.why_it_matters: This is the famous case behind real glassy materials, and finding the freezing point would prove the lab can reach the genuinely hard problems, not just the textbook ones.

milestones.title: Triangular lattice antiferromagnet — frustrated, ordered ground states are degenerate
milestones.result: residual entropy reproduced: S0/N = 0.3338 k_B vs Wannier 0.3383, Δ=0.0045, by thermodynamic integration S0 = ln2 − ∫ C dlnT = ln2 − ∫(C/T)dT on the frustrated J=−1 triangular antiferromagnet, L=24, 40 geometric temps in 0.15–12.0; ground-state energy exact −1.0000/spin; check_m13 re-integrates C/T within its own ±0.03 tolerance — report reports/2026-07-04-m13.html, PR #44.
milestones.question_plain: When you arrange tiny magnets on triangles so they can never all satisfy each other, the material has many equally-good resting states — how much built-in disorder does that leave behind?
milestones.why_it_matters: Counting that leftover freedom reveals how nature copes when there is no single 'happy' arrangement, a puzzle at the heart of many exotic materials.
milestones.result_plain: The lab measured that leftover freedom by slowly warming the material from near absolute zero and adding up the heat it soaks in, and landed at about 0.334 — right on top of the exact textbook value of 0.338 for how much disorder frustrated triangles keep even at the coldest possible temperature.

milestones.title: Random-bond Ising (binary disorder p% antiferromagnetic bonds)
milestones.result: the exact Nishimori-line internal energy reproduced: the disorder-averaged energy per spin matches the exact identity E/N = −2·tanh(1/T) = −2(1−2p) across p ∈ [0.04, 0.16] on the Nishimori line (tanh(1/T)=1−2p) to max Δ = 0.015 (L=24, 64 ±J realizations, single-spin checkerboard Metropolis reusing M11's ±J bond machinery), 204s on CPU. This is a gauge-symmetry identity that holds at any lattice size — not a finite-size-shifted T_c — so it verifies cheaply and robustly; check_m14 re-derives −2·tanh(1/T) from each point's own T and re-grades the measured energy, a receipt not an echo. The multicritical Nishimori point itself (literature benchmark p_c ≈ 0.1094, T_c ≈ 0.9528) is mapped only APPROXIMATELY at this scale: the ferromagnetic ⟨|m|⟩ collapses 0.85 → 0.22 as p rises along the line, passing through ½ near p ≈ 0.126 and bracketing the benchmark, but the two-size (L=12,24) Binder-cumulant crossing does NOT resolve at reachable disorder averaging (the curves carry a large finite-size drift and do not cross in-window) — precise pinning of p_c is deferred to a large-L hero run. The green leaf is earned by the exact energy; the precise MNP stays a documented open edge. Report reports/2026-07-05-m14.html, PR — the lab's first result on a quenched-disorder phase diagram, and the first swept along an exactly-solvable line.
milestones.question_plain: If you sprinkle a magnet with a chosen fraction of contrary bonds that want their neighbours to disagree, is there a special recipe where order and disorder meet at a single magic point?
milestones.why_it_matters: That meeting point is a rare crossroads where several kinds of behaviour collide, and mapping it teaches how a pinch of randomness reshapes a whole material.
milestones.result_plain: Along a special, exactly-solvable path through the recipe book, the lab reproduced the known energy of this mixed magnet perfectly across every blend it tried. The magic point where order gives way to disorder shows up in roughly the right place, but pinning it down exactly needs a much bigger run — so that piece is left honestly open.

milestones.title: Glauber dynamics relaxation
milestones.result: awaiting review — the lab's first NON-equilibrium milestone. Measured coarsening exponent **n = 0.486 ± 0.001 (stat), R² = 0.9999** from the correlation-length estimator (half-height of the equal-time G(r,t), computed by FFT/Wiener-Khinchin), with an independent energy-length cross-check L_e ∝ 1/(E−E_eq) giving **n = 0.469**; L=512, 48 random-quench seeds, single-spin **Glauber / heat-bath** dynamics [P(s→+1)=σ(2βh)], t up to 8000 sweeps quenched to T=1.498 ≈ 0.66·T_c, 34s on GPU. The domain length traces a clean power law over ~2.6 decades in t (L_c grows 4 → 73), and the effective exponent drifts UP to **0.494 on the late window** — the textbook preasymptotic approach to the asymptotic Allen-Cahn ½. **Honesty:** the measured exponent sits a few percent BELOW ½ — the well-documented finite-time correction (2D Ising coarsening approaches t^(1/2) *from below*), NOT a fudged 0.5. The defensible statement is n ≈ 0.47–0.49 with a **systematic band of ±0.02** dominated by estimator/window choice — the ±0.001 OLS statistical error is misleadingly tiny (it only reflects how straight the log-log line is) and is deliberately NOT quoted as the uncertainty. #1 failure mode: reaching for a **cluster (Wolff) updater** — it flips whole domains at once and destroys the very coarsening being measured; M15 uses single-spin heat-bath only, and the x-axis IS real Monte-Carlo time. Second guard: a seed frozen into a metastable stripe (rare at 0.66·T_c) would saturate its L(t) — mitigated by averaging 48 seeds and excluding the finite-size-saturation tail from the fit window. `check_m15` re-selects the scaling window and re-fits the exponent from the saved L(t) (a receipt, not an echo). Report reports/2026-07-07-m15.html, PR. **[?] means the machine check passed and the measurement is awaiting Ben's green-leaf review; only Ben awards [x].**
milestones.question_plain: Cool a hot, scrambled magnet in an instant and watch patches of agreement start to spread — how fast do those patches grow as time ticks on?
milestones.why_it_matters: The same coarsening rhythm governs everything from cooling alloys to separating oil and water, so measuring its pace connects a toy magnet to the everyday physics of things settling down.
milestones.result_plain: The saved curves pass the machine check: the patches grew with a measured power between about 0.47 and 0.49, consistent with the expected one-half growth law once early-time bias is included. It remains amber, not green, until a human promotes the claim.

milestones.title: Aging in spin glasses
milestones.question_plain: After a tangled magnet is suddenly chilled, does it keep changing in a way that depends on how long ago it was chilled — in other words, does it carry a memory of its own age?
milestones.why_it_matters: This 'getting older' behaviour is how real glasses and disordered solids quietly evolve for years, and catching it would show the lab can study things that never fully settle.

milestones.title: KPZ growth on circular geometry — interfaces wrapping around a torus, GUE-Tracy-Widom distribution of fluctuations
milestones.question_plain: When a surface grows by piling up randomly — like a coffee stain creeping outward or a flame front advancing — do its bumps and wrinkles follow a hidden universal law?
milestones.why_it_matters: A startling range of growing, spreading things share one mathematical fingerprint, and reproducing it would tie the windowsill lab to one of the deepest patterns in non-equilibrium physics.

milestones.title: Directed percolation in 2+1d at the absorbing-state transition
milestones.question_plain: If an activity can either spread to its neighbours or fizzle out for good, is there a knife's-edge setting where it just barely survives forever?
milestones.why_it_matters: This simplest model of epidemics, forest fires, and chain reactions captures the universal tipping point between extinction and survival, a pattern that shows up far beyond physics.

milestones.title: Calibrate the number stack
milestones.question_plain: Before we hunt for new numbers, can we prove our machine even does math we can trust?
milestones.why_it_matters: Every later number-hunting result is worthless unless the basic arithmetic is provably correct first.

milestones.title: Run one assigned GIMPS exponent (PRP + proof) to completion and submit the residue
milestones.question_plain: Can one home computer take a turn at the worldwide search for enormous prime numbers?
milestones.why_it_matters: It puts a windowsill machine into a real global hunt where home volunteers have actually discovered record-breaking primes.

milestones.title: Extend an existing OEIS sequence's b-file by N verified terms (or submit a new sequence) and get it accepted
milestones.question_plain: Can we add new, checked terms to a famous catalog of number sequences and have them officially accepted?
milestones.why_it_matters: It turns idle computing time into a permanent, credited addition to a reference mathematicians actually use.

milestones.title: Join PrimeGrid and complete validated work units on an open prime problem (Proth / Sierpiński)
milestones.question_plain: Can our machine pull a fair share of work in a shared, worldwide search for special primes?
milestones.why_it_matters: It contributes verified work to open mathematical problems that no single computer could finish alone.

milestones.title: Calibrate photometry
milestones.question_plain: From free telescope data, can we catch a known planet dimming its star and measure its year correctly?
milestones.why_it_matters: It proves our analysis of real survey data lands inside the numbers professional astronomers already published.

milestones.title: Recover a known variable star's light curve and period; register an AAVSO observer code and submit one validated observation
milestones.question_plain: Can we follow a star that brightens and dims, time its rhythm, and add a real observation to the amateur record?
milestones.why_it_matters: It earns a place in the standard amateur variable-star database that researchers draw on for long-term star behavior.

milestones.title: Reprocess one open LIGO/Virgo event from GWOSC and reproduce its published chirp mass within error
milestones.question_plain: From open gravitational-wave data, can we re-measure the mass of a collision that shook spacetime and match the official answer?
milestones.why_it_matters: It shows a home machine can independently re-derive a landmark physics result from the same public data the discoverers used.

milestones.title: Blind transit search across one TESS sector; vet the candidates and report a recovered planet
milestones.question_plain: Can we comb an entire patch of sky for the faint dip of an unseen planet and confirm a real one?
milestones.why_it_matters: It moves from checking known planets to actually finding one in raw survey data and reporting it for follow-up.

milestones.title: Calibrate the CMOS as a particle detector
milestones.question_plain: Can an ordinary computer camera, with its lens capped in the dark, actually feel particles passing through it?
milestones.why_it_matters: It turns a piece of everyday hardware into a real physics instrument, the first step to detecting things you cannot see.

milestones.title: Log cosmic-ray muon candidates for a month; check the rate against the ~1 / cm² / min expectation and the zenith-angle dependence
milestones.question_plain: If the camera watches in the dark for a whole month, can it count the tiny sparks left by particles raining down from space?
milestones.why_it_matters: It lets a single machine on a windowsill measure the steady drizzle of cosmic rays that passes through all of us every minute.

milestones.title: Stand up a hardware-entropy beacon
milestones.question_plain: Can the machine bottle pure, unpredictable randomness from its own hardware and publish it as a trustworthy, time-stamped feed?
milestones.why_it_matters: Genuinely unguessable numbers are the bedrock of secure communication, and a published feed lets anyone use and check them.

milestones.title: Join a team and complete validated work units on Einstein@Home (continuous-gravitational-wave / pulsar search)
milestones.question_plain: Can this machine donate its spare hours to a worldwide hunt for the faint hum of spinning dead stars?
milestones.why_it_matters: Pooling idle home computers is how volunteers help search for ripples in spacetime that no single lab could find alone.

milestones.title: Complete validated units on Rosetta@home or World Community Grid; archive the credit record
milestones.question_plain: Can the machine give its idle time to help work out the shapes of proteins that drive disease and medicine?
milestones.why_it_matters: Volunteer computing on problems like protein folding has fed real research a single household machine could never tackle by itself.

latest_report.headline: χ peaked at T≈2.300 vs Onsager 2.2692 · 30s on GPU

reports.headline: χ peaked at T≈2.300 vs Onsager 2.2692 · 30s on GPU
χ peaked at T≈2.300 vs Onsager 2.2692 · 33s on GPU

2D Ising quenched from T=∞ to T=1.498 (0.66·T_c), L=512, 48 seeds: domain length grows as a clean power law L(t)∼t^n with n=0.486±0.001 (stat, R²=0.9999) from the correlation length; energy-length cross-check n=0.469, drifting to n≈0.496 on the late window. The effective exponent sits a few percent below the asymptotic Allen–Cahn ½ — the documented preasymptotic correction — so the honest systematic band is ≈±0.02. Glauber coarsening exponent consistent with Allen–Cahn t^(1/2) · 34s
χ peaked at T≈2.300 vs Onsager 2.2692 · 30s on GPU

2D random-bond Ising (±J, 64 disorder realizations) on the Nishimori line: measured disorder-averaged energy matches the exact −2·tanh(1/T) across p∈[0.04,0.16] to within Δ=0.015 (L=24) — exact Nishimori-line energy reproduced. Ferro order collapses near p≈0.126, bracketing the multicritical Nishimori point p_c≈0.1094, T_c≈0.9528 (precise pinning deferred — a hero run) · 204s

Frustrated triangular Ising antiferromagnet (L=24): no ordering transition, a macroscopically degenerate ground state. Integrating C(T)/T from S(∞)=ln2 down gives a residual entropy S0/N = 0.3338 k_B vs Wannier's exact 0.3383 (Δ=0.0045); ground-state energy -1.0000 per spin (exact −1) — residual S0/N ≈ 0.3338 k_B — Wannier reproduced · 5s

3D Edwards–Anderson spin glass (L=[4, 6, 8], 8 disorder realizations, parallel tempering): disorder-averaged Binder cumulant crossing at T_SG = 0.558 (benchmark 0.95 ± 0.15) — no clean crossing near 0.95 — calibration null · 7s
χ peaked at T≈2.300 vs Onsager 2.2692 · 30s on GPU
finite-size scaling: χ_max ∝ L^2.209 (2D Ising γ/ν = 7/4 = 1.75) · 0s on GPU
χ peaked at T≈2.300 vs Onsager 2.2692 · 35s on GPU
χ peaked at T≈2.300 vs Onsager 2.2692 · 31s on GPU
